turns.text: Hello Elva, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts slightly in my seat, glancing toward the baby monitor on the coffee table*

Thank you for being here. Honestly, I'm... I'm still processing everything, so bear with me if I'm a bit all over the place.

The pregnancy itself was straightforward—textbook, really. My midwife was fantastic, and I felt empowered by having that continuity of care. I was *that* person reading all the research, understanding the physiology, feeling prepared. I thought knowledge would equal confidence, you know?

*pauses, rubbing my eyes*

But then Leo arrived three weeks ago, and I realized that knowing the *why* behind things doesn't actually prepare you for the *feeling* of it. I keep second-guessing myself constantly. Is he getting enough? Why does my body feel like it's been through a war? Why do I feel this strange mix of overwhelming love and... panic?

*laughs, a bit shakily*

The hardest part has been the disconnect between what I *know* intellectually and what I'm *experiencing* emotionally. The pediatrician said his weight drop is totally normal, but seeing those numbers hit different when it's your baby. My midwife has been supportive, but sometimes I wonder if my constant tracking and analyzing is actually helping or just feeding my anxiety.

I think I expected the postpartum period to feel more... linear. Instead, it's chaotic and tender and terrifying all at once.
Tell me about the first days and weeks after the birth — what that time was like for you, physically and emotionally.

*takes a breath, eyes getting a little glassy*

The first night home was surreal. I remember sitting in our bedroom at like 2 AM, Leo latched on, and I was just... crying. Not sad crying—I couldn't even categorize it. Joy? Overwhelm? Hormones? All of it at once. My body felt like it didn't belong to me anymore. Still does, honestly.

*shifts uncomfortably*

Physically, I was shocked by how much it *hurt*. Nobody really talks about that part in a direct way. I knew intellectually about perineal trauma and healing, but experiencing soreness that bad when you're also sleep-deprived and trying to figure out breastfeeding—it's a lot. And the bleeding. I'd read about lochia, understood the mechanism, but seeing it was different.

The breastfeeding was my biggest emotional minefield. I was so convinced I'd be *good* at it because I understood the biology. When Leo was losing weight—even knowing it's normal—I spiraled. I was pumping, tracking every single wet diaper, analyzing his latch like I was in a lab. My midwife kept reassuring me, but my brain wouldn't accept it.

*voice gets quieter*

The exhaustion is what nobody can really prepare you for. It's not just tiredness. It's a bone-deep fog where you lose time. I'd look up from feeding him and suddenly three hours had passed.

But there were these moments—his little grunts, the smell of his head—that cracked through everything. Those moments kept me tethered.
Tell me about feeding your baby — how you made decisions about it, and what the experience was actually like.

*leans forward, speaking more intensely*

Okay, so this is where my brain became both my best friend and my worst enemy. I *knew* I wanted to breastfeed. I'd read the research on immune benefits, the neurodevelopmental outcomes, all of it. But I also made this decision from a place of... I don't know, intellectual conviction mixed with some pressure I was putting on myself.

*rubs face*

When we got home, I was obsessed with getting it "right." I was timing feeds down to the minute, counting wet diapers like my life depended on it, analyzing the color of his poop—seriously, I have a spreadsheet. A *spreadsheet*. And when his weight dropped those ten percent in the first week, I completely fell apart. My midwife kept saying it was textbook, totally normal, but I heard it as "your body isn't doing what it's supposed to."

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

The thing is, the actual *act* of breastfeeding—when I could quiet my mind enough to experience it—felt incredible. That skin-to-skin contact, the rhythm of his swallowing... it's primal in a way no amount of reading prepared me for. But then immediately after, I'd be checking supply concerns, wondering if my hindmilk had enough fat content.

*laughs, a bit self-consciously*

My midwife actually said to me, "Elva, you're amazing at this. Can you just *be* instead of constantly analyzing?" And she was right, but stopping that loop is harder than it sounds. I'm trying to trust my body, but trusting feels terrifying.
How did you decide how to feed your baby — and did you feel that decision was truly yours to make?

*pauses, looking down at my hands*

That's... a really good question, actually. And it makes me uncomfortable, which probably means I need to sit with it.

*takes a moment*

Honestly? I think I decided to breastfeed before I was even pregnant. It was this abstract ideal I'd built in my head—the "natural" choice, the scientifically optimal choice. I'd internalized all this messaging about what a "good mother" does, and somewhere in there, my actual *choice* got buried under what I thought I *should* want.

*voice gets quieter, more honest*

My midwife never pressured me. She was actually really supportive of whatever I wanted to do. But I felt this weight from... everywhere else. Society, the research I'd read, some part of myself that equated breastfeeding with being a "real" mother. Which is ridiculous, but there it is.

*looks up*

What's hard to admit is that I haven't actually *chosen* anything else as a real option. If breastfeeding became impossible tomorrow, I'd feel like a failure. That tells me my decision wasn't as autonomous as I'd like to believe. It was more like... I selected the path I was already supposed to be on.

*fidgets*

My husband asked me the other day if I was happy doing this, and I couldn't answer him directly. I deflected into data about nutrition. Because if I really examined whether this is *my* choice or just the choice I inherited... I don't know what I'd do with that answer right now.
Tell me about how you were feeling emotionally in the weeks and months after the birth — the full picture, not just the positive side.

*takes a shaky breath, eyes welling up*

Oh God. Okay. The full picture is... messy.

*wipes eyes*

Everyone asks how I'm doing, and I say "tired but good," because that's what you're supposed to say. But the truth is I'm terrified most of the time. Not of specific things—nothing medically wrong with Leo—just this constant, gnawing anxiety that I'm failing somehow. That my body isn't enough, that my milk isn't enough, that *I'm* not enough.

*voice cracks slightly*

There are moments where I look at Leo and feel this fierce, overwhelming love that physically hurts. And then fifteen minutes later, I'm sitting in the shower at 4 AM crying because I can't remember the last time I showered, and my nipples are cracked, and I haven't slept more than two hours straight in three weeks, and I don't recognize myself anymore.

*rubs face*

The identity thing is brutal. I was this person with a career, intellectual pursuits, autonomy. Now I'm defined by my output—literally. My body is a production facility, and I'm measuring its success by ounces and wet diapers. That's not poetic; that's depressing.

*pauses, voice lower*

And I feel guilty saying that. Like, I *wanted* this baby. I love him desperately. So why do I also feel... trapped? Why do I sometimes resent the constant demands on my body? Why can't I just be grateful?

*looks directly at you*

I don't think anyone's really asking how I'm actually doing.
Tell me about your physical recovery after the birth — what that process was like and what support you had.

*shifts uncomfortably, wrapping arms around myself*

The physical recovery has been... harder than I expected, honestly. I had a relatively "easy" delivery—no major complications, no interventions—so I think I underestimated how much my body would need to heal.

*speaks more quietly*

The soreness was intense for the first two weeks. Sitting hurt, walking hurt, everything hurt. I knew theoretically about perineal trauma, but experiencing it while also trying to function and care for a newborn is different. I couldn't even sit comfortably to breastfeed, which added to the whole anxiety spiral about whether I was doing it right.

*fidgets with hands*

The bleeding went on longer than I expected—I'd read about lochia, but seeing it week after week was unsettling. And my midwife checked in on me regularly, which helped, but I wish someone had been more explicit about what "normal" actually looks like day-to-day. Like, nobody told me about the night sweats, the hormone crashes, how long it takes to feel remotely human again.

*voice becomes more frustrated*

My husband has been supportive, but there's only so much he can do. My mom came for the first week, which was wonderful, but then she left and suddenly I was managing everything—recovery, feeding, the emotional weight—mostly alone during the day while he's working.

*pauses*

The hardest part isn't the physical pain, though. It's that nobody acknowledges how much your body feels like it's not yours anymore. It's still being used, still producing, still not your own.
Tell me about how your sense of yourself changed after you became a parent — your identity, your relationships, your daily life.

*goes quiet for a long moment, staring out toward the window*

I don't know who I am anymore. And that terrifies me more than any medical complication ever could.

*voice wavers slightly*

Before Leo, I had this identity wrapped up in being competent, knowledgeable, productive. I worked full-time in a field I loved. I read constantly, I had conversations with adults about ideas that mattered to me. I had... autonomy. Control.

*laughs bitterly*

Now I'm covered in spit-up, I can't complete a sentence without being interrupted, and the most intellectual thing I do is obsess over milk composition. And intellectually, I *know* that this is temporary, that my brain will come back online eventually. But knowing that doesn't make it less devastating to experience.

*rubs eyes*

My relationship with my husband is strained. He's trying—he really is—but he gets to go to work and exist in the world. Meanwhile, I'm tethered to this baby 24/7. We barely touch anymore except when I'm asking him to take the baby so I can shower. The intimacy we had... it's just gone. And I resent him for not understanding how isolating this is, even though intellectually I know that's not fair.

*voice gets smaller*

My friendships have evaporated. They text, but they have lives. They're doing things. I'm... here. In this loop of feeding and diaper changes and anxiety spirals.

*looks at you directly*

The worst part? I feel guilty for feeling this way. Like I should just be grateful and content, and the fact that I'm not makes me a bad mother.
Were there parts of your previous self — your work identity, social life, sense of independence — that felt hard to hold onto?

*nods slowly, eyes getting distant*

Oh God, yes. All of it.

*leans back*

I loved my work. I was good at it—the kind of good where you feel competent and valued. And I had to step back because, logistically, childcare costs were prohibitive, and there's this unspoken expectation that I'd be the one to... adjust. My husband's career didn't pause. Mine did.

*voice tightens*

What's maddening is that I *chose* it on some level. We made a financial decision together. But choosing something doesn't make it feel less like a loss. I went from having meetings and projects and intellectual stimulation to... asking my husband if I can have twenty minutes alone to take a walk. That shift happened so fast I'm still reeling from it.

*fidgets*

And my friendships—they didn't end because my friends were unsupportive. They ended because I literally can't leave the house without orchestrating military-level logistics. I can't be spontaneous. I can't just *be* a person outside of motherhood for more than a couple hours.

*voice cracks slightly*

The hardest part is that I *should* be fine with this. Culturally, motherhood is supposed to be enough. It's supposed to fulfill me completely. And it does, in these intense, beautiful moments. But it's also suffocating.

*looks down*

I'm terrified that if I voice this too loudly, people will think I regret having Leo. I don't. But I also grieve who I was. And nobody really talks about that grief being okay to feel.
Tell me about returning to work — or thinking about returning to work — and what that transition was like for you.

*laughs, a short, hollow sound*

I haven't returned yet. I'm still in this limbo of maternity leave, and honestly, I don't even know what "returning" looks like right now.

*shifts uncomfortably*

Before Leo was born, I thought I'd be back at work by now, or at least have a clear plan. But the reality is... I can't afford childcare. Not on what I'd be making. So I'm home, and my career is just... paused. Indefinitely, maybe.

*voice becomes more agitated*

What nobody tells you is how that kills your sense of self in real time. My employer has been understanding about it, but every week that passes, I feel more disconnected from that version of myself. My skills are atrophying. I'm not in meetings anymore, not contributing to projects. I'm Googling "why is my baby grunting at night" instead of doing the work I was trained for.

*rubs face*

And there's this guilt—constant guilt. Because I *should* be grateful to be home with Leo, right? I should be relishing this time. Some part of me does. But another part is resentful and bored and desperate to feel like a competent adult again in a professional context.

*voice quieter*

My husband keeps saying "once Leo's older, you can go back," like that's supposed to comfort me. But I don't know if I *can* go back. The gap will be there. And I'll be competing with people who never left.

*looks up*

I'm grieving a future I thought I'd have.
Tell me about how you used digital tools, apps, forums, or online communities after the birth — what role they played in your life as a new parent.

*laughs, shaking head*

Oh, the apps. God, the apps.

*pulls out phone*

I have... let me see... a feeding tracker, a sleep tracker, a growth percentile calculator, a diaper log. I'm in three different Facebook groups for postpartum mothers, two Reddit communities about breastfeeding, and I'm subscribed to like five different lactation consultant blogs.

*sets phone down, looking sheepish*

The thing is, they felt necessary at first. When I was spiraling about Leo's weight drop, I could scroll through hundreds of other mothers saying "this happened to me and everything was fine." There's something comforting about that collective experience, you know? Like you're not alone in your panic.

*voice becomes more critical*

But they also became this... rabbit hole. I'd be up at 3 AM comparing my milk supply to someone else's, reading horror stories about tongue ties, watching videos of "proper" latch technique. And instead of calming me down, the information just fed the obsession. Every app notification felt like a reminder that I should be tracking more, analyzing more, optimizing more.

*rubs temples*

The online communities are a double-edged sword. They validate my feelings, which is huge. But they also normalize this level of scrutiny and anxiety. Everyone's measuring everything, everyone's worried, everyone's comparing. It's like we're all drowning together and calling it support.

*looks up*

My midwife finally said, "Elva, put the phone down. Your body knows what to do." But honestly? Trusting my body feels more foreign than trusting the data.
Tell me about how you would describe your overall experience of maternity care — from preconception through to now. What would you want future services to know?

*takes a long breath, seeming to gather thoughts*

My care was actually really good, clinically speaking. My midwife was attentive, knowledgeable, and genuinely supportive. The pregnancy was managed well. The birth itself was empowering. So from a purely medical standpoint, I can't complain.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

But there's this gap between good *clinical* care and care that actually addresses what it means to become a mother. Nobody prepared me for the identity loss. Nobody talked about the possibility that I might feel trapped by the thing I chose. Nobody acknowledged that being smart and knowledgeable doesn't protect you from feeling completely lost.

*voice becomes more passionate*

My midwife would check my physical recovery, ask about feeding, monitor Leo's weight. But when I was crying in her office about my career being on pause, she kind of... pivoted back to the clinical stuff. Not because she was unsupportive, but because that's not really her role, I guess.

*leans forward*

What I needed—what I think all new mothers need—is someone asking the harder questions. Not just "are you bleeding too much?" but "who are you becoming in this transition?" Not just "is he gaining weight?" but "are *you* okay with what this is costing you?"

*voice quieter*

I also wish someone had validated that it's possible to love your baby deeply and still grieve your old life. Those two things can coexist. And acknowledging that doesn't make you ungrateful or a bad mother.

*looks directly at you*

Future care needs to hold space for the messy emotional reality, not just the biological one.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits quietly for a moment, then nods slowly*

Yeah, there is something.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

I keep coming back to this idea of trust. I built my entire approach to motherhood on understanding the *science* of it—like if I knew enough, controlled enough, analyzed enough, I could prevent bad outcomes and feel secure. And what I'm realizing is that was never really about Leo's wellbeing. It was about managing my own anxiety.

*looks down at hands*

The irony is that all that knowledge has actually created distance between me and my son. I'm so busy measuring his wet diapers and tracking his feeds that sometimes I miss just... being with him. Feeling him. My midwife said something that's been haunting me in the best way: "Your body knows what to do. Your baby knows what to do. Maybe you just need to get out of the way."

*voice cracks slightly*

I'm terrified of that. Of surrendering control. Of trusting my instincts when they might be wrong. But I'm also starting to understand that the most important thing I can give Leo isn't perfect data or optimal nutrition—it's a mother who's present instead of constantly in her head.

*pauses*

And I think maternity care, at least for someone like me, needs to somehow help women untangle what we *think* we should be doing from what actually serves us and our babies. Because I had excellent clinical care, but I needed someone to help me understand that my hypervigilance wasn't protection—it was a cage.

*looks at you*

Does that make sense?
